jsonLdWeb.name: Chicken Egg Calculator
jsonLdWeb.description: Calculate chicken egg production based on flock size, breed type, hen age, and seasonal adjustments with feed cost comparison.

mainEntity.name: How many eggs does a chicken lay per year?

acceptedAnswer.text: High-production breeds (Leghorns, ISA Browns) lay 280-320 eggs per year. Heritage breeds (Plymouth Rock, Orpington) lay 150-200. Bantam breeds produce 100-150 eggs per year. Production declines about 10-15% each year after the first.

mainEntity.name: Do chickens lay fewer eggs in winter?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, egg production drops about 40% in winter due to shorter daylight hours. Chickens need 14-16 hours of light to maintain peak production. Adding a coop light on a timer can help maintain winter production.

mainEntity.name: How much feed does a laying hen eat per day?

acceptedAnswer.text: A laying hen eats about 1/4 to 1/3 pound (4-5 oz) of feed per day, or roughly 1.5 lbs per week. At $15-$20 per 50-lb bag, that costs about $0.06-$0.08 per hen per day.

mainEntity.name: At what age do hens start laying?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most hens start laying at 18-22 weeks (4.5-5.5 months). High-production breeds tend to start earlier. Heritage breeds may not start until 24-28 weeks. The first eggs are often small and irregular.

mainEntity.name: How long do chickens keep laying eggs?

acceptedAnswer.text: Chickens lay productively for 2-3 years. After year one, production drops 10-15% annually. Most hens continue laying some eggs for 5-7 years, but at greatly reduced rates.

mainEntity.name: Is it cheaper to raise chickens for eggs?

acceptedAnswer.text: Feed costs average $0.06-$0.08 per hen per day. A good layer produces eggs costing about $1.50-$2.50 per dozen in feed. Store eggs cost $3-$6 per dozen, so backyard eggs can save money once startup costs are covered.

howItWorks:
How the Chicken Egg Calculator Works

This calculator estimates egg production based on breed potential, adjusted for age and seasonal light changes.

Daily Eggs = Hens × (Breed Rate ÷ 365) × Age Factor × Season Factor
  • High production: 280-320 eggs/hen/year (0.82-0.88/day)
  • Heritage: 150-200 eggs/hen/year (0.41-0.55/day)
  • Bantam: 100-150 eggs/hen/year (0.27-0.41/day)
  • Age decline: ~15% drop per year after year 1
  • Winter drop: ~40% reduction without supplemental light
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

How many eggs does a chicken lay per year?

High-production breeds lay 280-320 eggs per year in their first year. Heritage breeds produce 150-200 eggs, and bantams lay 100-150. Production drops about 15% each year after the first.

Do chickens lay fewer eggs in winter?

Yes, production drops about 40% in winter due to shorter days. Hens need 14-16 hours of light for peak laying. Adding a coop light on a timer can maintain production through winter months.

How much feed does a laying hen eat per day?

A laying hen eats about 1/4 pound (4 oz) of feed daily, roughly 1.5 lbs per week. At typical feed prices, this costs about $0.06-$0.08 per hen per day.

At what age do hens start laying?

Most hens begin laying at 18-22 weeks old. High-production breeds start earliest, while heritage and bantam breeds may wait until 24-28 weeks. First eggs are often small and become regular over a few weeks.

How long do chickens keep laying eggs?

Hens lay productively for 2-3 years. After the first year, production declines about 15% annually. Most hens still lay occasionally for 5-7 years but at much lower rates.

Is it cheaper to raise chickens for eggs?

Feed costs work out to roughly $1.50-$2.50 per dozen eggs, compared to $3-$6 at the store. Once you recover startup costs (coop, feeders, chicks), backyard eggs save money and are fresher.
